**Deportivo Independiente Medellín**, also known as **Independiente Medellín** or **DIM**, is a Colombian professional [football club](/wiki/Football_club_(association_football) "Football club (association football)") based in [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín") that currently plays in the [Categoría Primera A](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) They play their home games at [Estadio Atanasio Girardot](/wiki/Estadio_Atanasio_Girardot "Estadio Atanasio Girardot"), which seats 40,943 people, and is also shared with city rivals [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional"). The team is dubbed "El Poderoso de la Montaña" (Mighty of the Mountain) due to Medellín's geographical location high in the [Andes Mountains](/wiki/Andes_Mountains "Andes Mountains"), and as a reference to the many amateur titles it won in its early years.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Founded in 1913 as the second oldest club in Colombia, Independiente Medellín has won the [Categoría Primera A](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A") six times: in [1955](/wiki/1955_Campeonato_Profesional "1955 Campeonato Profesional"), [1957](/wiki/1957_Campeonato_Profesional "1957 Campeonato Profesional"), [2002–II](/wiki/2002_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2002 Categoría Primera A season"), [2004–I](/wiki/2004_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2004 Categoría Primera A season"), [2009–II](/wiki/2009_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2009 Categoría Primera A season"), and [2016–I](/wiki/2016_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2016 Categoría Primera A season"), and the [Copa Colombia](/wiki/Copa_Colombia "Copa Colombia") three times: in [1981](/wiki/1981_Copa_Colombia "1981 Copa Colombia"), [2019](/wiki/2019_Copa_Colombia "2019 Copa Colombia"), and [2020](/wiki/2020_Copa_Colombia "2020 Copa Colombia"). Its best performance at international level was in [2003](/wiki/2003_Copa_Libertadores "2003 Copa Libertadores"), when the team reached the semifinals of the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/Copa_Libertadores "Copa Libertadores").

Independiente Medellín has a rivalry with Atlético Nacional, and the teams face each other in [El Clásico Paisa](/wiki/El_Cl%C3%A1sico_Paisa "El Clásico Paisa"), which is considered one of the most important derbies in the country.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

Independiente Medellín was founded on 14 November 1913 under the name of *Medellín Foot Ball Club* by siblings Alberto, Luis, and Rafael Uribe Piedrahíta.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The team played its first match against Sporting Medellín, who defeated them 11–0.

The club won amateur titles in 1918, 1920, 1922, 1923 and 1930; with these titles it became one of the most important teams of Colombia at the time.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1948, Medellín joined professional football and played the [first edition](/wiki/1948_Campeonato_Profesional "1948 Campeonato Profesional") of the Colombian professional league. Medellín placed seventh out of 10 teams, winning seven matches. Their first match was a 4–0 defeat against [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali"). Their first win was 3–2 against [Junior](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Junior "Atlético Junior").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### 1950–1954: "La Danza del Sol"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1950–1954: \"La Danza del Sol\"")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Deportivo_Independiente_Medellin_1922.jpg/330px-Deportivo_Independiente_Medellin_1922.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deportivo_Independiente_Medellin_1922.jpg)

The 1922 amateur squad proudly showcasing the coveted national cups earned in 1918 and 1920

In 1950, Medellin signed many Peruvian footballers in a movement known as [El Dorado](/wiki/El_Dorado_(football) "El Dorado (football)"), when Colombian teams signed many foreign footballers. Some of the players signed include brothers [Constantino Perales](/wiki/Constantino_Perales "Constantino Perales") and [Agapito Perales](/wiki/Agapito_Perales "Agapito Perales"), [Segundo Castillo](/wiki/Segundo_Castillo_(footballer,_born_1913) "Segundo Castillo (footballer, born 1913)"), [Roberto Drago](/wiki/Roberto_Drago "Roberto Drago") and [Luis Guzmán Gonzales](/wiki/Luis_Guzm%C3%A1n_(Peruvian_footballer) "Luis Guzmán (Peruvian footballer)"). This squad was dubbed "La Danza del Sol", and [that season](/wiki/1950_Campeonato_Profesional "1950 Campeonato Profesional") the club finished fourth with 34 points.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

Medellín did not play in 1952 and 1953 due to economic problems. In 1953, the club changed its entire administration and was renamed to its current name, *Deportivo Independiente Medellín*.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) In the club's first season back, the [1954 season](/wiki/1954_Campeonato_Profesional "1954 Campeonato Profesional"), they finished third in the league table. 1954 also marked the arrival of Argentine footballer [José Manuel "El Charro" Moreno](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Manuel_Moreno "José Manuel Moreno"), who had won two South American championships with Argentina and many trophies at River Plate.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

### 1955–1970: First Golden Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1955–1970: First Golden Age")]

Under El Charro's command, who had taken a role as a player-manager, DIM won its first title in the [1955 Campeonato Profesional](/wiki/1955_Campeonato_Profesional "1955 Campeonato Profesional"), finishing first with 31 points and just one defeat. Argentine striker [Felipe Marino](/w/index.php?title=Felipe_Marino_(footballer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Felipe Marino (footballer) (page does not exist)") was the tournament's top goalscorer, with 22 goals.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) The team won its second title two years later, in [1957](/wiki/1957_Campeonato_Profesional "1957 Campeonato Profesional"), with almost the same players as the previous seasons. [José Vicente Grecco](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Vicente_Grecco "José Vicente Grecco") was the top scorer of the tournament. Despite having strong squads, in [1959](/wiki/1959_Campeonato_Profesional "1959 Campeonato Profesional") and [1961](/wiki/1961_Campeonato_Profesional "1961 Campeonato Profesional") DIM finished runner-up to [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C."), who had one of the strongest sides in Colombian football history and won four straight titles from 1960 to 1964; in 1964, DIM also came close to winning the title, finishing third but only three points from Millonarios.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

In 1966, Medellín achieved their first ever qualification for the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/1967_Copa_Libertadores "1967 Copa Libertadores"), after finishing runner-up in the league. They played against Argentine sides [Racing de Avellaneda](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Avellaneda "Racing Club de Avellaneda") and [River Plate](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_River_Plate "Club Atlético River Plate"), Bolivian teams [31 de Octubre](/wiki/Club_31_de_Octubre "Club 31 de Octubre") and [Bolívar](/wiki/Club_Bol%C3%ADvar "Club Bolívar"), and fellow Colombians [Independiente Santa Fe](/wiki/Independiente_Santa_Fe "Independiente Santa Fe"). They finished fifth out of six in their group and were eliminated.

### 1971–1983: Lacklustre campaigns, Copa Colombia title

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1971–1983: Lacklustre campaigns, Copa Colombia title")]

In 1971, Medellin did not participate in the league due to economic hardships. The 70s were a low point for the club; the club were consistently finishing in the middle to bottom half of the league table, and were never close to winning the title.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

The club won its first Copa Colombia in 1981; although this title is not officially recognized by many experts nor by [DIMAYOR](/wiki/DIMAYOR "DIMAYOR"), it is recognized by [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Despite the joy of winning their first cup, in the [1981 league season](/wiki/1981_Campeonato_Profesional "1981 Campeonato Profesional"), the club had a lacklustre season and finished last in the aggregate table with 38 points from 47 matches.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) Things improved for the rest of the 80s, and despite not winning anymore titles for the decade, the club was consistently qualifying for the octogonal finals.

### 1984–1998: Slow Improvements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1984–1998: Slow Improvements")]

In [1984](/wiki/1984_Campeonato_Profesional "1984 Campeonato Profesional"), with young manager [Julio Comesaña](/wiki/Julio_Comesa%C3%B1a "Julio Comesaña") at the post, the club finished third in the octogonal, the best participation the club had in the league since 1964.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Deportivo_Independiente_Medellin_1928.jpg/330px-Deportivo_Independiente_Medellin_1928.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deportivo_Independiente_Medellin_1928.jpg)

Medellín in 1928

#### 1989: cancelled season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1989: cancelled season")]

In 1989, a year where Medellín had one of the strongest squads in the league and was expected to win the title, a tragic event occurred. In one of the final games of the season, Medellín tied [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali") 0–0 at home. During the game, linesman [Álvaro Ortega](/w/index.php?title=%C3%81lvaro_Ortega&action=edit&redlink=1 "Álvaro Ortega (page does not exist)") disallowed a Medellín goal, angering many people.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Reports say that a person that had been sent by [Pablo Escobar](/wiki/Pablo_Escobar "Pablo Escobar") hunted Ortega down and murdered him, with the motive being a lost bet on the match. In response, the [Colombian Football Federation](/wiki/Colombian_Football_Federation "Colombian Football Federation") decided to cancel the rest of the season, which left the [1989 league season](/wiki/1989_Campeonato_Profesional "1989 Campeonato Profesional") without a winner.[[14]](#cite_note-:3-14)

#### 1993: Heartbreaking runner-up season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1993: Heartbreaking runner-up season")]

On 19 December 1993, going into the last match of the [1993 season](/wiki/1993_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "1993 Categoría Primera A season"), Medellín and [Junior](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Junior "Atlético Junior") were fighting for a tight first place, as both clubs had the same number of points. Junior was playing [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali") at home in [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla") while simultaneously Medellín played hometown rivals [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional"). A Medellín win combined with a Junior loss or draw would give Medellín the title. At halftime América were leading the game in Barranquilla 1–0 and in Medellin the game was still 0–0; at that moment América were winning the title due to the draw in Medellín. Junior scored two goals to put the game at 2–1 with ten minutes remaining, and Medellín scored at the same time to put the game at 1–0. América tied the game at 2–2 with seven minutes remaining. The match at Estadio Anatasio Girardot ended with Medellín winning 1–0 while awaiting the 2–2 game in Barranquilla to end, which still had five minutes remaining due to a delay at the start of the second half. Medellín players were celebrating with a victory lap and giving interviews with reporters white they waited for the final whistle in Barranquilla. However, [Oswaldo Mackenzie](/wiki/Oswaldo_Mackenzie "Oswaldo Mackenzie") scored a late goal in the 89th minute and gave Junior the 3–2 win and the title, leaving the Medellín players and fans heartbroken.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

As a result of their great campaign, Medellin returned to the Copa Libertadores for the first time in almost three decades. Entering the [1994 edition](/wiki/1994_Copa_Libertadores "1994 Copa Libertadores"), they topped their group and then beat [Universitario](/wiki/Universitario "Universitario") in the round of 16 before being eliminated by Junior in the quarter-finals.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

In the 1994 league season, Medellin had a great season and qualified to the Cuadrangular final, a group of four teams with one of them being rivals Nacional. Going into the last match of the season, Medellin did not have any chance at winning the title, but a win or draw would prevent Nacional from achieving the title; however the match ended 1–0 in favor of *Nacional* and Nacional won the title.[[18]](#cite_note-:5-18)

### 1999–2009: End of title drought

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1999–2009: End of title drought")]

*El Poderoso* had a great [1999 season](/wiki/1999_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "1999 Categoría Primera A season"), finishing in the top eight of both Apertura and Finalización tournaments, and finishing fourth in the aggregate table. This season was different from the standard format; in the Apertura tournament there were no playoffs. The Finalizacion tournament had playoffs, where Medellin topped their group and qualified for the Finalizacion finals against rivals Nacional, with the winner playing the Apertura tournament champion in a championship final to decide the 1999 league champion. Medellin lost the Finalizacion finals to Nacional 1–0 on aggregate, and Nacional went on to win the league title against Améric, while Medellin missed out on a spot for the [2000 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2000_Copa_Libertadores "2000 Copa Libertadores").[[18]](#cite_note-:5-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Independiente_Medellin.JPG/330px-Independiente_Medellin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Independiente_Medellin.JPG)

[Barra brava](/wiki/Barra_brava "Barra brava") supporters *Rexixtenxia Norte* at [Estadio Atanasio Girardot](/wiki/Estadio_Atanasio_Girardot "Estadio Atanasio Girardot")

Medellin came close to winning their third league title in [2001](/wiki/2001_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2001 Categoría Primera A season") thanks to [Jorge Serna](/wiki/Jorge_Serna "Jorge Serna")'s prolific goalscoring, who finished as top scorer tied with Carlos Castro on 29 goals. The club ended up losing the final to [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali") 3–0 on aggregate, although they reached the final in an unexpected manner; in the regular season they finished in 10th place and occupied the last seed for the eight teams that qualified for the playoffs through the aggregate table.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

After 45 long years of agony, Medellín won its third league title in the [2002 Finalización](/wiki/2002_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2002 Categoría Primera A season") tournament under manager [Víctor Luna](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Luna "Víctor Luna"), who replaced [Reinaldo Rueda](/wiki/Reinaldo_Rueda "Reinaldo Rueda") halfway through the season after he was sacked due to poor results. Medellín played against [Deportivo Pasto](/wiki/Deportivo_Pasto "Deportivo Pasto") in the two-legged final. *El Rojo Paisa* beat Pasto 2–0 at home in the first leg with goals from Robinson Muñoz and an own goal from Julio César Valencia. In the second leg on 23 December 2002, Medellín drew 1–1 away from home, with [Mauricio Molina](/wiki/Mauricio_Molina "Mauricio Molina") scoring Medellín's goal from a free-kick, helping the team lift the trophy after a 3–1 aggregate score.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The 2002 league title gave the club a spot in the [2003 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2003_Copa_Libertadores "2003 Copa Libertadores"), where they qualified for the knockout stages by topping their group with twelve points, which consisted of [Boca Juniors](/wiki/Boca_Juniors "Boca Juniors"), [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona_S.C. "Barcelona S.C."), and [Colo-Colo](/wiki/Colo-Colo "Colo-Colo"). During the group stage, the club famously beat [Bianchi](/wiki/Carlos_Bianchi "Carlos Bianchi")'s Boca Juniors, 1–0. "Medallo" beat [Cerro Porteño](/wiki/Cerro_Porte%C3%B1o "Cerro Porteño") on penalties in the round of 16 and [Grêmio](/wiki/Gr%C3%AAmio_Foot-Ball_Porto_Alegrense "Grêmio Foot-Ball Porto Alegrense") in the quarter-finals to reach the semi-finals for the first time in its history, with their opponent being [Santos](/wiki/Santos_FC "Santos FC"). In the first leg played at [Estádio Urbano Caldeira](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Urbano_Caldeira "Estádio Urbano Caldeira"), DIM lost 1–0. In the second leg at home, [Tressor Moreno](/wiki/Tressor_Moreno "Tressor Moreno") scored first to level the aggregate score at 1–1, but the club eventually lost the game 3–2 (4–2 on aggregate) and was eliminated, narrowly missing out for the final, which would have been played against their group stage opponent, Boca Juniors.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In the 2004 Apertura, Medellín and Nacional qualified for the finals, setting up the first "Paisa" finals in the league's history. The final was played over two legs, both at Atanasio Girardot: in the first leg, Medellín won 2–1 with goals scored by [Rafael Castillo](/wiki/Rafael_Arlex_Castillo "Rafael Arlex Castillo") and Jorge Serna. The second leg was played on 27 June; it ended 0–0 and Medellín became the champion of the [2004 Apertura](/wiki/2004_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2004 Categoría Primera A season"), its fourth league title, won under manager [Pedro Sarmiento](/wiki/Pedro_Sarmiento "Pedro Sarmiento").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[18]](#cite_note-:5-18) In the 2004 Finalizacion, the club also made the playoffs, but finished third in its group and were eliminated.

In the [2005 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2005_Copa_Libertadores "2005 Copa Libertadores"), the club topped their group, which was made up of [Atletico Paranaense](/wiki/Club_Athletico_Paranaense "Club Athletico Paranaense"), América de Cali, and [Libertad](/wiki/Club_Libertad "Club Libertad"). They also unexpectedly beat Paranaense 4–0 away in [Curitiba](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba")[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) on their way to the round of 16, where they faced [Banfield](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_Banfield "Club Atlético Banfield") and lost 5–0 on aggregate.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

In the [2008 Finalización](/wiki/2008_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2008 Categoría Primera A season"), the club almost won its fifth title, but lost the final to América de Cali with [Santiago Escobar](/wiki/Santiago_Escobar "Santiago Escobar") as head coach. The next season, the 2009 Apertura, was very poor; the team finished in last place. However, in the [2009 Torneo Finalización](/wiki/2009_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2009 Categoría Primera A season"), with the departure of Santiago Escobar as head coach, his assistant, [Leonel Álvarez](/wiki/Leonel_%C3%81lvarez_(footballer,_born_1965) "Leonel Álvarez (footballer, born 1965)"), replaced him, and the team got its fifth title, beating [Atlético Huila](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Huila "Atlético Huila") 3–2 on aggregate. In that season, [Jackson Martinez](/wiki/Jackson_Martinez "Jackson Martinez") broke the league's top scoring record with 18 goals (the previous record was [Léider Preciado](/wiki/L%C3%A9ider_Preciado "Léider Preciado")'s 17 goals),[[29]](#cite_note-29) a record that was broken again later by [Cortuluá](/wiki/Cortulu%C3%A1 "Cortuluá") forward [Miguel Borja](/wiki/Miguel_Borja "Miguel Borja") in 2016, with 19 goals.

### 2010–present: Back-to-back runner-ups and sixth league title

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2010–present: Back-to-back runner-ups and sixth league title")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/El_Equipo_Del_Pueblo.JPG/330px-El_Equipo_Del_Pueblo.JPG)](/wiki/File:El_Equipo_Del_Pueblo.JPG)

[Tifo](/wiki/Tifo "Tifo") by the Medellín fans which reads "El Equipo del Pueblo" (The People's team) before a league fixture against Once Caldas in 2014

During the 2010s, DIM was close to winning league titles several times. In the [2012 Finalizacion](/wiki/2012_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2012 Categoría Primera A season"), they qualified to the finals with a last minute goal from [Ray Vanegas](/w/index.php?title=Ray_Vanegas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ray Vanegas (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ray_Vanegas "es:Ray Vanegas")] against [Itagui Ditaires](/wiki/Itagui_Ditaires "Itagui Ditaires"). Their rival in the finals was [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C."), and after a 1–1 draw on aggregate, DIM lost on penalties.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In 2014, they finished as runners-up to [Independiente Santa Fe](/wiki/Independiente_Santa_Fe "Independiente Santa Fe") in that year's Finalización tournament. Six months later they made the final again, this time losing to [Deportivo Cali](/wiki/Deportivo_Cali "Deportivo Cali"). These losses were finally overcome in the [2016 season](/wiki/2016_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2016 Categoría Primera A season"), where the club won its sixth league title. In the Apertura tournament, they finished first in the regular season table with 40 points. Then they eliminated Deportivo Cali and [Cortuluá](/wiki/Cortulu%C3%A1 "Cortuluá") in the playoffs to set up a final with Junior; the first leg in [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla") ended 1–1 and the second leg was won by Medellín 2–0, with [Christian Marrugo](/wiki/Christian_Marrugo "Christian Marrugo") scoring a brace and securing a 3–1 aggregate victory.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

With the 2016 league title, *El Poderoso* gained a spot in the [2017 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2017_Copa_Libertadores "2017 Copa Libertadores"), returning to the tournament for the first time since 2010. They were placed in Group 3 along with [River Plate](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_River_Plate "Club Atlético River Plate"), [Emelec](/wiki/C.S._Emelec "C.S. Emelec"), and [Melgar](/wiki/FBC_Melgar "FBC Melgar"). The club placed third in the group and was transferred to the [Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/2017_Copa_Sudamericana "2017 Copa Sudamericana"), where they eventually lost to [Racing Club](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Avellaneda "Racing Club de Avellaneda") in the second round. One of the highlights of their Copa Libertadores run was beating powerhouse River Plate 2–1 at [Estadio Monumental](/wiki/Estadio_Monumental_Antonio_Vespucio_Liberti "Estadio Monumental Antonio Vespucio Liberti").[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main article: [El Clásico Paisa](/wiki/El_Cl%C3%A1sico_Paisa "El Clásico Paisa")

Medellín's greatest rivalry is with the city's other major club, [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional"), in a match known as *Clásico Paisa* ([Paisa](/wiki/Paisa_(region) "Paisa (region)") derby). Both clubs share the same stadium; Atanasio Girardot. Atlético Nacional has a clear advantage over Independiente Medellín in titles won, with 35 titles (most in Colombia) to Medellín's 9 titles. However, Medellín defeated Nacional in the first final series played between both, in the [2004 Apertura](/wiki/2004_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2004 Categoría Primera A season"). This was considered as an upset, since Nacional's squad had a much higher value that Medellín's.

The rivalry is especially strong due to each team's main fanbases; Rexixtenxia Norte for Independiente Medellín and Los Del Sur for Atlético Nacional. There are often fights between these two fanbases, which is why sometimes only the fanbase of one team is allowed entry.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) The two clubs are named with the location that they occupy in the stadium; Rexixtenxia Norte occupy the northern section and Los Del Sur occupy the southern section.

The first *Clásico Paisa* was played on 12 September 1948, where Medellín beat Nacional 3–0. Over 300 matches have been played between the two clubs, with Nacional dominating the historical record by 40 wins.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The club also has minor rivalries with other clubs in the [Medellín Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn_Metropolitan_Area "Medellín Metropolitan Area"), such as [Envigado](/wiki/Envigado_F.C. "Envigado F.C."), [Águilas Doradas](/wiki/%C3%81guilas_Doradas "Águilas Doradas"), and [Leones](/wiki/Itag%C3%BC%C3%AD_Leones_F.C. "Itagüí Leones F.C."). Although none of these teams have won Primera Division titles, matches between them still draw large crowds due to their close geographical location
